title: Getters and Setters 🚪
description: Control who is allowed to read and edit your object's data.

slides.title: Maximum Security 🛡️
slides.content:
By default, objects are maximum security data fortresses.

You cannot write to an Instance Variable from the outside, and you cannot read it either! Your script has no idea `@name` exists inside the `Dog` instance.

If you try `puts fido.name`, Ruby will crash!

slides.title: Getter Methods 👓
slides.content:
If you want the outside world to be able to read an object's internally stored data, you must write a "Getter" instance method to hand it out.

```ruby
class Dog
  # ... initialize stuff
  def name
    @name 
  end
end
```

Now, `puts fido.name` cleanly calls this method, which safely implicitly returns `@name`.

slides.title: Setter Methods 📝
slides.content: If you want the outside world to be able to UPDATE (or "set") the data, you must build a Setter method using a special syntax: `def name=()`
slides.codeSnippet:
class Dog
  def name=(new_name)
    @name = new_name
  end
end

fido = Dog.new
# Ruby calls the setter method under the hood
fido.name = "Sparky"

quizData.question: Why can you not simply run `puts my_car.@model` to print an object's internal variable?

options.text: Because Instance Variables are intensely secure and completely invisible to outside code by default.

quizData.explanation: If you want the outside world to read an object's internal data, you must explicitly construct a Getter Method that acts as a front door.

slides.title: A Better Door🚪
slides.content:
# The `attr_accessor`

Writing Getter and Setter methods for every single variable gets extremely tedious.

Ruby solves this with magic! If you place `attr_accessor` at the top of your class with a symbol, Ruby automatically generates the Getter and Setter methods for you invisibly!

slides.title: Accessor Magic
slides.content: Run this! We didn't write any getter or setter methods, yet `attr_accessor` automatically gave us full read and write abilities from the outside.
slides.codeSnippet:
class Dog
  attr_accessor :name
  
  def initialize(start_name)
    @name = start_name
  end
end

bad_boy = Dog.new("Fido")
# Writing to it (Setter)
bad_boy.name = "Spike"
# Reading from it (Getter)
puts bad_boy.name

quizData.question: What specifically does `attr_accessor :age` do inside a Class?

options.text: It turns the Class into a Lambda.
It automatically writes the invisible Getter and Setter instance methods so outside code can seamlessly read and edit `@age`.

quizData.explanation: It stands for Attribute Accessor. It creates the doors so you don't have to.

slides.title: Readers and Writers
slides.content:
Don't want them to edit it? Use `attr_reader` (creates read-only Getters).
Don't want them to read it? Use `attr_writer` (creates write-only Setters).
